title: DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly
exclusions: Does NOT audit the DSAR fulfillment clock (covered by 22.36.1); focuses on upstream identity verification that can legitimately or illegitimately slow fulfillment.
evidence: Saved search 'dsar_verification_friction' (daily) identifies subjects with ≥3 failed verifications in a 14-day window and reports to the DPO review queue.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.12(6) allows controllers to ask for identity confirmation but repeated failures can indicate a design that frustrates rights — the UC surfaces the pattern.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of GDPR Art.12(6) — Splunk UC-22.36.4: DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.4' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA requires a verifiable consumer request process; excessive friction is an enforcement pattern.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of CCPA §1798.130(a) — Splunk UC-22.36.4: DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.4' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.12(6) (parent assurance='partial'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of UK GDPR Art.12(6) — Splunk UC-22.36.4: DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A single subject_token with four failed identity verifications in 14 days appears in the output the next day.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A new-ticket flow with one correctly-matched verification does not appear.

dataSources: DSAR platform identity-verification events (OneTrust, TrustArc, ServiceNow Privacy), IdP step-up audit logs, manual review tickets.
spl:
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar OR sourcetype=servicenow:privacy action=verification earliest=-14d
| eval pass=if(result="success",1,0), fail=if(result="failure",1,0)
| stats sum(pass) as passes sum(fail) as failures values(ticket_id) as ticket_ids by subject_token
| where failures>=3 AND passes<1
| table subject_token failures ticket_ids
description: Surfaces data-subjects whose right-exercise attempts repeatedly fail verification, which — aggregated over time — is a reliable indicator either of a broken verification UX or of a deliberate design that frustrates rights. Either interpretation is a DPO priority.
value: Takes the single most common enforcement narrative ('the verification process itself denied the right') and turns it into a measurable metric.
implementation: (1) Ensure DSAR verification events emit subject_token and result; (2) run daily; (3) feed findings to a DPO review dashboard; (4) after a fixed threshold, escalate via SOAR for legal review.
visualization: Bar chart of verification failure rate per channel, table of repeat-failure subjects, single-value 'repeat-failure subjects (14d)'.
knownFalsePositives: Genuine impersonation attempts will (correctly) produce multiple failures for the same subject_token; pair with IdP risk signals before escalating.
grandmaExplanation: Takes the single most common enforcement narrative ('the verification process itself denied the right') and turns it into a measurable metric — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: DSAR platform identity-verification events (OneTrust, TrustArc, ServiceNow Privacy), IdP step-up audit logs, manual review tickets..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ensure DSAR verification events emit subject_token and result; (2) run daily; (3) feed findings to a DPO review dashboard; (4) after a fixed threshold, escalate via SOAR for legal review.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar OR sourcetype=servicenow:privacy action=verification earliest=-14d
| eval pass=if(result="success",1,0), fail=if(result="failure",1,0)
| stats sum(pass) as passes sum(fail) as failures values(ticket_id) as ticket_ids by subject_token
| where failures>=3 AND passes<1
| table subject_token failures ticket_ids
```

Understanding this SPL

**DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly** — Takes the single most common enforcement narrative ('the verification process itself denied the right') and turns it into a measurable metric.

Documented **Data sources**: DSAR platform identity-verification events (OneTrust, TrustArc, ServiceNow Privacy), IdP step-up audit logs, manual review tickets. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: dsar; **sourcetype**: onetrust:dsar, servicenow:privacy. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=dsar, sourcetype=onetrust:dsar, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pass** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by subject_token** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where failures>=3 AND passes<1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **DSAR identity-verification friction — failed-verification anomaly**): table subject_token failures ticket_ids


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart of verification failure rate per channel, table of repeat-failure subjects, single-value 'repeat-failure subjects (14d)'.
